summary: Joel Spolsky dissects Microsoft Passport, a single sign-on system that he argues is fundamentally a scheme to build the world's largest consumer database by circumventing the cookie-based privacy protections built into web browsers. He explains how cookies normally protect users by only being sent back to their originating domain, and how Passport uses client redirects to subvert this golden rule, allowing any Passport-affiliated site to share user information through Microsoft as a gatekeeper. Spolsky warns that Microsoft plans to embed Passport directly into Internet Explorer and Windows, eliminating the last technical barrier to pervasive cross-site tracking. He argues that Microsoft's privacy promises are meaningless given the inevitability of security breaches, unscrupulous employees, court orders, and corporate bankruptcies that expose user data.
key_points: Cookies have a 'golden rule' that protects privacy: they are only sent back to the originating web domain, preventing cross-site information sharing
Microsoft Passport uses client redirects to circumvent cookie security, allowing any Passport-affiliated site to share user data through Microsoft as a central broker
The network effect makes Passport exponentially more valuable as more sites join — the value grows as the square of participating sites
Microsoft plans to embed Passport into IE and Windows itself, removing the last technical friction protecting user privacy
Corporate privacy promises are unreliable because of security breaches, employee abuse, court orders, and bankruptcy-driven data sales
money_quotes: It seems to me like a fairly blatant attempt to build the world's largest, richest consumer database, and then make fabulous profits mining it.
The golden rule of cookies is that they are only sent back to the same web domain as they came from.

The way Passport uses client redirect to subvert cookie security is basically just taking advantage of a security hole in web browsers. Cookies weren't meant to allow this. But you can bet that this is one security bug that Microsoft is not going to fix.
The spooky thing about Passport is that there's one company that serves as the gatekeeper to joining the network: Microsoft.
The best way to lie is through repeated assertion until eventually nobody notices the lie.
Even the best laid plans to protect consumer's privacy don't work. There are always software bugs and security goof-ups.

If Microsoft was honest about protecting your privacy, they would let users keep their private information on their own computers, and they would ask you every time they were going to reveal some data.
key_insight: Single sign-on systems that centralize identity through one corporate gatekeeper don't just solve a convenience problem — they dismantle the architectural privacy protections that cookies were designed to enforce.
